video_title: The 100,000 Sandbox Problem — Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
gist: Modal CTO Akshat Bubna traces the company's evolution from a Kubernetes-alternative 'self-provisioning runtime' into today's agent infrastructure platform, covering the 100,000-sandbox RL bursty-compute problem, a capital-light 17-neocloud strategy, open-sourced speculative decoding work (Dflash) upstreamed to SGLang, and why Modal is betting on 'agent experience' over developer experience as the next infra moat.

themes.theme: Kubernetes pain to self-provisioning runtime (AX vs DX)

insights.claim: Modal's founding thesis was that workflow orchestration tools were painful specifically because they had to run on Kubernetes, which is hard to manage, not built for burstiness/custom images, and has a terrible developer experience — so Modal built a decorator-based 'self-provisioning runtime' instead.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Frames Modal's entire product bet as a rejection of Kubernetes-centric infra for bursty AI/ML workloads — relevant for evaluating Modal vs. Kubernetes-based competitors.
insights.quote: why are workflow orchestration products so hard to use? It's because you have to run them on Kubernetes. Kubernetes is hard to manage. It's not built for burstiness and um custom images and has a terrible developer experience.

insights.claim: Modal added GPU support to its product roughly a year before ChatGPT launched, initially targeting classical inference workloads like computer vision and XGBoost rather than language models, and did not anticipate how large the GPU/LLM opportunity would become.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Shows Modal's GPU/inference business emerged opportunistically rather than as a deliberate LLM bet, useful context for assessing how 'accidental' the current AI-infra platform positioning is.
insights.quote: we added GPUs to the product a year before Chat GPT came out. We just didn't think it would be that big of a deal.

insights.claim: Modal has shifted its SDK team's mandate from optimizing 'developer experience' (DX) to optimizing 'agent experience' (AX), arguing that coding agents benefit from the same code-first, decorator-based primitives as humans do — e.g., an agent can make a couple of typed decorator changes instead of parsing hundreds of untyped Kubernetes YAML files.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Suggests infra vendors whose config is typed, code-native, and collocated with application logic will have a structural advantage as coding agents become primary infra 'users' — a lens for evaluating AI-infra platforms broadly.
insights.quote: why would you have an agent read through hundreds of Kubernetes files and like write YAML that's not even typed when it can basically make a couple changes in a decorator and it gets this sort of self-provisioning runtime

insights.claim: Even as fewer humans read raw code, Modal has found that observability (dashboards, CLI-accessible logs/metrics) is becoming more important, not less, because humans still need to interpret agent-driven system behavior and make judgment calls even when agents do their own investigation via the CLI.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Counters the narrative that observability tooling becomes less valuable as agents write and manage code — implies durable demand for observability infrastructure even in an agent-heavy stack.
insights.quote: we push a lot of it to the CLI so the agents can do their own investigation, but you still need humans to go interpret what's going on and uh you know, make judgment calls and whatnot.

insights.claim: Modal built its sandbox product in May 2023, before the market recognized 'agent sandboxes' as a category, with the first published example being an early coding agent ('smart developer') placed in a self-iterating loop.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Modal had a ~2-year head start building sandbox infrastructure before agentic coding demand materialized, which is a durable moat if switching costs or performance advantages compound.
insights.quote: we built sandboxes in May of 2023 before anyone was even knew this was going to be a thing. And the first example we published was uh we took small developer and put in a loop uh so the agent can iterate on itself.

insights.claim: During reinforcement-learning rollouts, customers sometimes need up to 100,000 sandboxes simultaneously, making RL workloads far more bursty than typical steady-state agent usage.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of infra demand created by RL post-training pipelines — a concrete data point for sizing GPU/compute-infra demand tied to the RL training trend.
insights.quote: you sometimes need 100,000 sandboxes.

insights.claim: Reinforcement-learning rollout workloads are 'insanely bursty' — more bursty than either steady agent execution or standard sandbox usage — requiring infrastructure that can scale far more elastically than typical agent workloads.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Investors tracking GPU-cloud or elastic-compute providers should weight RL/post-training demand as a distinct, spikier workload class from inference or standard agent hosting.
insights.quote: sandboxes are uh except when you're doing RL. RL is insanely bursty.

themes.theme: Inference economics: autoscaling, snapshotting, speculative decoding

insights.claim: Modal's core competitive differentiation versus other inference platforms (implicitly including providers like Fireworks, Together, and Base10) is autoscaling capability, not raw GPU access, because it is not universally true that competing platforms can autoscale effectively.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Suggests investors evaluating inference-hosting companies should weight elastic autoscaling engineering as the key differentiator rather than treating GPU-hosting as commoditized.
insights.quote: the thing that we we have actually specialized in is the autoscaling aspect. uh because we we we found that it's not universally true that everyone else can autoscale

insights.claim: Modal incorporated GPU state snapshotting — including snapshotting torch-compiled models — into its product so that cold starts happen much faster after scaling down to zero.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: GPU snapshotting is a concrete technical lever that reduces the cost penalty of scale-to-zero, making elastic (rather than always-on) GPU capacity more economically viable.
insights.quote: we've incorporated GPU snapshotting into the product so you can actually uh take the GPU state uh like your torch compiler model snapshot it and next cold starts way faster

insights.claim: In speculative decoding, the dominant lever for inference speed is the draft model's 'accept length' (how many predicted tokens the larger model verifies and accepts), which can deliver a 2-4x multiplicative speedup with no quality loss, whereas low-level kernel optimizations alone only yield a few percentage points of improvement.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Investors and builders assessing inference-cost claims should look at accept-length/draft-model quality rather than kernel-level marketing claims when evaluating real throughput gains.
insights.quote: improving kernel only give you like a few percentage points of improvement and increasing accept length literally is a multiplicative decrease in like 2 to 4x without much head-on performance.

insights.claim: Modal has open-sourced 'Dflash,' a block-based speculative decoder that predicts a block of tokens at a time rather than one token at a time, as part of its inference-optimization work.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete open-source artifact investors/engineers can evaluate to benchmark Modal's inference performance claims against alternatives.
insights.quote: we've been working a bunch on Dlash which is a block based speculator. Uh so it's um uh instead of predicting uh one token at a time it's predicting a block and we we've been open sourcing our work with it.

insights.claim: Modal's inference strategy is to open-source and upstream its performance work (working closely with the SGLang team) so outside users can get the same performance as proprietary providers using open-source tooling, while Modal monetizes primarily through elasticity, scaling, and reliability rather than by gatekeeping the optimization IP itself.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Signals a go-to-market pattern where infra vendors compete on operational/elasticity moats rather than proprietary model-serving code, which compresses margins for pure inference-optimization plays.
insights.quote: we've taken the approach of open sourcing our contributions and upstreaming them we work closely with the SG lang team

insights.claim: Modal's 'auto endpoints' let users deploy an open-source model through a no-code UI/CLI flow that already bakes in Modal's inference optimizations (like Dflash), while still allowing users to 'eject' into full code-level control once they need to fine-tune or customize.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Describes a product wedge strategy — low-friction entry with a path to deeper platform lock-in as customers scale sophistication — relevant to modeling Modal's expansion revenue dynamics.
insights.quote: sometimes people actually don't want to touch the code and they want to get started with an endpoint that works and has all the great performance and uh scalability that modal has

themes.theme: Capital-light cloud strategy and compute hedging

insights.claim: Modal runs on a capacity pool spanning 17 different cloud providers and neoclouds rather than owning its own data centers, betting that staying capital-light and focusing on the software/reliability layer is its real differentiator versus companies building out physical data center capacity.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A capital-light, multi-cloud-arbitrage model is a distinct investment thesis versus vertically integrated GPU-cloud/data-center players — implies different capex, margin, and risk profiles to model.
insights.quote: We don't have our own data centers. We just run across a lot of NeoClouds and providers.

insights.claim: Because reliability varies significantly across the many neocloud GPU providers Modal uses, Modal built its own reliability layer so that failures (e.g., a GPU 'falling off the bus') don't affect user workloads, which in turn lets Modal use far more of the available low-reliability capacity than an end user could access directly.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: This reliability-abstraction layer is a concrete technical moat that lets Modal monetize cheaper, less-reliable capacity that competitors or end users can't safely use directly.
insights.quote: that's why something we've invested a lot of time in is actually uh building our own reliability layer on top. Uh so if the GPU falls off the bus or something happens we uh user workloads are not affected

insights.claim: Modal has a dedicated 'compute strategy' team that manages financial-style hedging decisions around GPU capacity, including the blend of one-year versus three-year reservations, capacity forecasting, and bets on how the GPU supply chain will evolve — a discipline the conversation compares to airlines hedging fuel costs.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Highlights that GPU-cloud unit economics depend heavily on procurement/hedging skill, not just raw scale — a factor investors should probe when evaluating GPU-cloud margins and moats.
insights.quote: there's a lot of interesting financial questions of like uh like what is the blend between one year and three year reservations? How do we forecast our own capacity?

insights.claim: Modal is building a cheaper 'batch tier' for inference that returns results within roughly 24 hours for latency-insensitive workloads, and finds that demand for this comes mostly from non-LLM companies (e.g., computational biology) rather than from mainstream LLM use cases, aside from some eval and synthetic-data-generation workloads.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Suggests batch/discounted-latency pricing tiers (similar to frontier labs' batch APIs) are underused for LLMs specifically but have real demand in adjacent scientific-compute verticals — a potential underexploited pricing lever.
insights.quote: The demand that we see for something like that is actually not for LMS. Uh although sometimes people want to run evals and do synthetic data prep

insights.claim: Modal built a private overlay network using IPv6 addresses that lets Modal containers within the same workspace address each other privately; it was originally built to support the key exchange needed to set up RDMA networking for distributed training, but users have since repurposed the underlying TCP overlay network for unrelated use cases Modal never documented.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Illustrates how low-level networking primitives built for one purpose (training) become general-purpose infrastructure users adopt in unplanned ways — a sign of platform depth and stickiness.
insights.quote: this like overlay network using IPv6 addresses. Uh so if modal containers uh within the same workspace uh when this enabled can actually address each other using this private IP v6 address uh and no one else can

insights.claim: Modal's multi-node distributed training product provides roughly 3 terabytes per second of internal RDMA networking bandwidth between GPU nodes, which the company describes as the standard needed for this class of workload.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete bandwidth benchmark investors can use to compare Modal's distributed-training networking against dedicated training-cluster providers using InfiniBand/Mellanox interconnects.
insights.quote: we have I think like 3 terb per second uh internal networking which is the standard that's needed

insights.claim: Modal explicitly does not target large-scale foundation-model pretraining runs; its multi-node training product is built for smaller-scale post-training work, such as post-training mid-size Qwen-family models, where elasticity matters more than the raw interconnect scale needed for frontier pretraining clusters.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Clarifies Modal's competitive lane is the elastic, smaller-scale post-training/RL market rather than competing with mega-scale pretraining clusters — useful for segmenting the AI-infra competitive landscape.
insights.quote: we're not going for obviously like large scale pre-training runs. Uh the thing that we've built multiode training for is uh we see a lot of u smaller scale post-training. uh like uh people are post training like mediumsiz quen models

insights.claim: Modal has automated much of its own field/solutions-engineering work through an internal tool called 'auto inference,' an agent harness that runs GPU profiling (e.g., Nvidia's Nsight profiler), sweeps configurations, and can autonomously migrate a customer's workload from one GPU generation to another (e.g., H200 to B200).
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete, working example of 'auto research'/agentic infra optimization already in production internally, not just hype — a signal that AI-driven infra ops automation is real and monetizable now.
insights.quote: we've automated our own FTE efforts using uh this harness which is um the agent will just spin up a sweep of different things. Uh it'll change your GPUs from H200 to B200 and actually works really well.

insights.claim: Despite industry hype around 'auto research,' what Akshat Bubna has actually observed from customers is that current automated research tools operate at the level of hyperparameter sweeps guided by model intuition, not true architecture-level innovation or neural architecture search.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations for 'AI doing AI research' narratives — current state is efficient hyperparameter search, not autonomous architectural discovery, which matters for how investors price 'self-improving AI' theses.
insights.quote: the stuff I've seen people do with it is nowhere on the architecture level. It's pretty much tweaking parameters, but it's it's basically a hyperparameter sweep that's guided by some sort of model intuition.

insights.claim: Coding models crossed a capability threshold around Claude 4: prior-generation models could not reliably write working Modal infrastructure code, but current models can now 'one-shot' correct Modal code out of the box.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete, vendor-observed capability jump tied to a specific model generation — useful evidence for tracking when frontier coding models became reliable enough for real infra-as-code tasks.
insights.quote: I think like pre-Cloud 4 they were not and then now they're able to oneshot uh stuff out of the box.

insights.claim: Modal is building an internal benchmark, 'Modal Bench,' specifically to identify tasks where coding agents still fail — such as interpreting observability data and logs to diagnose why something is failing — so Modal can turn those gaps into new CLI-accessible primitives or 'skills.'
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Shows a feedback loop where agent failure modes directly drive product roadmap (new CLI surface area), a repeatable pattern for how AX-first infra vendors should evolve their products.
insights.quote: So, so we have a model skill now that which is kind of actually why we built this model bench. It's to find things like that so we can we we can address them in our tuning skill.

themes.theme: Competitive landscape: Replicate, Gitpod, and managed agent platforms

insights.claim: Akshat Bubna's postmortem on former competitor Replicate is that offering a plain model-inference API mainly serves a hobbyist market that is much less sticky than serving companies that need to build deeper, more customized products — which is why Modal deliberately avoided a pure model-API business model.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A direct competitive lesson: commodity model-API businesses face weak retention versus code-first platforms serving differentiated production use cases — relevant to valuing model-hosting startups.
insights.quote: This is one thing we've kind of stayed away from is providing an API for models because I think providing model APIs is some of it ends up serving like a really hobbyist market which is much less sticky

insights.claim: Akshat Bubna's read on Gitpod (whose team joined OpenAI, reportedly informing Codex Cloud) is that Gitpod was technically strong but bet on 'buildtime sandboxes' rather than 'runtime sandboxes' — and in retrospect runtime sandboxes turned out to be the better-shaped product for the agentic use case that ended up taking off.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific architectural choice (buildtime vs. runtime sandbox configuration surface) as the deciding factor in a real competitive outcome — a concrete framework for evaluating other sandbox/agent-infra startups.
insights.quote: it's just like buildtime sandboxes versus runtime sandboxes and actually it turned out runtime was better

insights.claim: Modal is structurally bullish on the CI/CD infrastructure market because increased use of coding agents will drive far more CI runs, and much of current CI time is wasted on preparing dependencies/artifacts — a problem Modal believes memory snapshot-and-restore primitives can solve more efficiently.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Flags CI/CD as an adjacent growth market for elastic-compute/snapshotting infra vendors as agentic coding volume scales — a potential expansion vector for Modal and comparable platforms.
insights.quote: we're very bullish at modal on the CI market as well because as there's more agents uh coding agents they're going to run a lot more CI and the primitives there can be much better

insights.claim: Modal deliberately takes no opinion on which agent 'harness' or meta-harness platform wins (citing examples like Vercel's and Databricks' Omni-style agent platforms), instead positioning itself as the infrastructure layer underneath whichever harness a customer chooses to run in a Modal sandbox — reflecting the view that Modal benefits regardless of which harness wins as long as it consumes more infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A 'picks-and-shovels' positioning that is largely agnostic to which agent-harness layer wins the market — relevant for assessing Modal's risk exposure to harness-layer competition.
insights.quote: everything's bullish modal as long as it consumes more infra. That's why we're focusing on the infra layer.

insights.claim: Ramp runs both its internal accounting agent and its external-facing customer agent on Modal, which Akshat Bubna cites as evidence that production-grade agents need direct control over compute primitives (file handling, snapshotting, networking) that managed agent platforms from Anthropic, OpenAI, or Gemini don't natively expose.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Suggests managed agent platforms and specialized sandbox/compute infra are complementary rather than substitutes once companies move from prototyping to production-grade agents — relevant for sizing the addressable market beneath managed-agent products.

insights.claim: Modal embeds its own engineers directly with fast-moving customers — for example, sending an engineer to work inside Cognition full-time — because the latency of remote communication was too high otherwise, a level of hands-on customer support that Akshat Bubna contrasts with how hyperscalers like AWS engage with startups.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Highlights a service-intensity moat (embedded engineering support) that large cloud providers structurally can't replicate at Modal's customer count, aiding retention with frontier AI-native customers.
insights.quote: We we sent him over because uh the latency of communication was too high otherwise.

insights.claim: Frontier customers are already experimenting with continual/online learning on top of Modal's primitives, but Modal is deliberately waiting to see how the workload shape evolves before building dedicated continual-learning products.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Flags continual learning as an emerging, not-yet-mainstream training paradigm to watch — early customer experimentation on general-purpose infra often precedes dedicated product categories.
insights.quote: they are taking the primitives we have um and trying to use them in very interesting ways like continue learning. It's possible as the stuff gets better some of that will be part of uh you know our offering as well

insights.claim: Modal sees computational biology/drug discovery and robotics (companies running live robot deployments) as major non-LLM growth verticals, which diversifies its business away from being purely an LLM-inference platform.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Suggests AI-infra demand growth is broadening beyond LLM inference into bio and robotics, a diversification signal relevant to sizing the total AI-infra TAM beyond chatbot/LLM use cases.
insights.quote: we work a lot of companies that are doing things like drug discovery and computational bio like the chai discoveries world big things are probably going to happen there

insights.claim: AI/ML workload compute ratios have shifted from roughly 8:1 GPU-to-CPU historically to closer to 1:1 today, because agentic workloads constantly call out to CPU-heavy operations, meaning infrastructure providers must collocate GPU and CPU capacity rather than treating them as separately scaled resources.
insights.speaker: host, citing Nvidia's Jensen Huang / GTC keynote
insights.so_what: A structural shift in compute ratio requirements favors infra providers (like Modal) built for tight GPU/CPU collocation over providers optimized for GPU-only or CPU-only scaling.
insights.quote: what we're calling like the inference inflection right that let's say in AI workloads or machine learning workloads it used to be like let's call 8:1 GPU to CPU and now it's more like 1:1

insights.claim: A prediction floated from the xAI/Grok team (relayed on the show) is that the next tier of video generation will not be a single bigger text-to-video model but an agent — a language-model backbone that can use tools and write code — that orchestrates multiple video-model calls to produce longer-form video; Modal's guest confirms seeing early customers building exactly this kind of video-manipulation agent on Modal's GPU sandboxes.
insights.speaker: host, relaying a prediction from Ethan (xAI/Grok team); confirmed by Akshat Bubna
insights.so_what: If correct, this shifts value in video AI toward orchestration/agent layers built on GPU sandbox infra rather than toward ever-larger single video foundation models — relevant for positioning in the video-gen stack.

insights.claim: Modal's TypeScript SDK sees disproportionately heavy usage for agent and sandbox workloads compared to Python, because builders working on agent infrastructure are typically not doing machine learning themselves, whereas Python remains dominant for training and inference use cases.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: Indicates a language-ecosystem split forming between agent/application builders (TypeScript) and ML/training builders (Python), useful for tooling and developer-platform investment decisions.

insights.claim: Modal has no air-gapped or on-premises offering and is cloud-only, which forecloses use cases requiring air-gapped or on-prem deployment (e.g., certain regulated or government customers) for now.
insights.speaker: Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
insights.so_what: A clear boundary on Modal's addressable market — investors modeling Modal's TAM should exclude on-prem/air-gapped segments unless this changes.
insights.quote: No, we we cloud only.
